emergent_themes.description: Students with extreme commuting situations develop artificial delivery dependence that they rationalize as convenience choice rather than necessity
Students maintain identity as 'savers' or 'price-conscious' while actually spending heavily on convenience, creating cognitive dissonance
Students with prior gig work experience show more empathy toward delivery workers but also more strategic platform usage
Temporary or unstable living situations drive food delivery behavior more than traditional student budget constraints

coding_summary: Adam represents an extreme case of delivery dependence driven by his challenging commute situation. Despite claiming to be 'really picky on money' and a 'saver,' he spends over €100 weekly on food delivery, revealing a significant gap between stated values and actual behavior. His prior experience as a delivery rider gives him unique insights into platform differences and rider challenges. He demonstrates sophisticated platform arbitrage, using Uber for chain restaurants and Deliveroo for independents. His post-graduation plans show awareness that his current delivery dependence is situational, expecting to cook more but increase grocery delivery when he moves to Dublin permanently.
coverage_gaps: RO3_emotional_social.is_there_stigma_around_solo_ordering_or_ - Adam shows no evidence of stigma around solo ordering; his delivery habits are primarily solo and driven by practical necessity rather than social concerns
